ECON1220_ch7_17:
Explanatory Note – Summary
IF rent ceiling can be effectively enforced by the 
government, then P’ would be the rental payment that 
would be paid by buyers and received by the sellers, 
and Qs’ will be the no. of apartment available in the 
market 
Qs’ < Q*, then DWL happens (reduction in social 
welfare as measured size of social surplus)
Since price mechanism has been restricted by the 
government, non-price rationing methods will appear 
to “solve” the shortage problem. If such non-price 
rationing methods do not allocated apartments to 
highest WTP buyers, size of social surplus would 
further reduce (further loss on top of DWL) 
ECON1220 L1 – Market, Demand and Supply
17
ECON1220_ch7_18:
Explanatory Note – Summary
However, we know that government’s enforcement is 
never perfect, Black/Grey market appears as a non-
price rationing method under the existence of shortage
At Qs’, the “black market payment + P’ “ can be as 
large as the WTP of buyers, which mean:
Black market payment transfer surplus back to sellers (If 
just a redistribution of surplus, it does not affect efficiency)
Highest WTP buyers will “win” by (biggest amount) black 
market payment, and be allocated with apartments (non-
price rationing method, but eventually is related to 
price/money)
Therefore, black market payment allows efficient 
allocation of apartment (highest WTP buyers), though 
surplus will be largely transferred into sellers’ hands

ECON1220_ch7_19:
Explanatory Note – Summary
Think further, with black/Grey market payment, the 
rental received by sellers could be larger than P’, so 
quantity supplied of apartment could be higher than 
Qs’ , therefore DWL reduced (closer to Q*, improve of 
efficiency)
In extreme case, if government does nothing to enforce 
the rent ceiling, P* and Q* could still be the market 
outcome
Black market serves as a mean to improve the 
efficiency of allocation of apartments under rent 
control 
Another example:  iPhone 8 (resell to mainland by 
those Hong Kong buyers who won the “lucky-draw” by 
Apple

ECON1220_ch7_37:
Taxation
Tax incidence: the division of the burden of a 
tax between buyers and sellers
When an item is taxed, its price might rise by 
the full amount of the tax, by a lesser amount, 
or not at all:
If the price rises by the full amount of the tax, 
buyers pay the tax
If the price rises by a lesser amount than the tax, 
buyers and sellers share the burden of the tax
If the price doesn’t rise at all, sellers pay the tax

ECON1220_ch7_68:
Question (2) - Answer
A. Breakfast cereal or sunscreen
Breakfast cereal has more close substitutes 
than sunscreen, so demand for breakfast 
cereal is more price-elastic than demand for 
sunscreen.  
So, a tax on breakfast cereal would cause a 
larger DWL than a tax on sunscreen.
ECON1220 L1 - Government meets Market
68
ECON1220_ch7_69:
Question (2) - Answer
B. Hotel rooms in the short run or hotel rooms 
in the long run
The price elasticities of demand and supply 
for hotel rooms are larger in the long run than 
in the short run. 
So, a tax on hotel rooms would cause a 
larger DWL in the long run than in the short 
run.

Groceries are more of a necessity and 
therefore less price-elastic than meals at 
fancy restaurants.
So, a tax on restaurant meals would cause a 
larger DWL than a tax on groceries.

provide all kinds of welfare to its citizens will

growing rapidly

Demand-Supply framework is convenient!

and equilibrium into two groups: (1) Demand-
side and (2) Supply-side

determines the so-called equilibrium, which is 
a simple concept and easy to work, however:

sellers; (ii) Among buyers; and (iii) Among 
sellers are the “forces” behind the scene

human thinking and behavior behind

How Price Allocates Resources
Demand-supply model describes how price is 
determined in a free-market
“What price does” is ALSO important: 
Allocation of scarce resources
Who get the scare goods : In market, scarce 
goods go to “most willing to pay” buyers (for 
whom to produce)
Final product market provides sellers incentive on 
what they produce and factor markets on how to 
produce
That is the three fundamental questions of 
economics!

means three fundamental economics 
questions are solved without a “Commander”
Everyone just perform whatever to maximize 
their own benefits in the market

Policy Discussion
Does free-trade benefit everyone?
In our example, we have Wing and Albert, 
and specialization and trade benefit both 
Wing and Albert
How about if we talk about trade between two 
countries?  Does free-trade benefit everyone 
in both countries?

For example, when China trades with US
Both countries benefited
ECON1220 L1 – Interdependence and the Gains from Trade

ECON1220_ch3_65:
Policy Discussion
Trade is the flow of goods/services between 
two countries; on the other hand, it is also 
just like both countries open its boarder and 
allow people of both countries competing with 
each others
In this sense, if China’s comparative 
advantage is in labor-intensive items (due to 
low labor cost), trade will put US labors to 
compete with the low-cost labors of China
ECON1220 L1 – Interdependence and the Gains from Trade
65
ECON1220_ch3_66:
Policy Discussion
Therefore, although US, as a whole unit, can 
be benefited from obtaining “cheap” imports 
(less costly than producing by itself), but 
some US labors who facing the competition 
from China’s labors may get hurt
However, those US labors, who get hurt in 
labor market, can also enjoy the low-cost 
imports in their consumption at the same time
Therefore, considering the two impacts, it is 
difficult to conclude those US labors are 
benefited or not

Outlines
This chapter is about some methodology 
issues in economics and in scientific analysis

ECON1220_ch2_4:
Cause and Effect
Correlation: Two things always appear at the 
same time (When A happens, you can 
observe B happens as well)
However, it does not necessary mean that A 
causes B to happen
ExamplE: You observe than an object weights 
less at the top of a high mountain, at where 
temperature is low.  Will you conclude that 
temperature affects the weight of an object?
ECON1220 L1 - Thinking Like an Economist
4
ECON1220_ch2_5:
Cause and Effect
Causality: A makes B happen
ExamplE: Gravity makes an object weights less 
at the top of a high mountain
Causality and Correlation
To conclude the appearance of “causality” from A 
to B, we have to be much restrictive in our 
analysis
ECON1220 L1 - Thinking Like an Economist
5
ECON1220_ch2_6:
Cause and Effect
HOLD OTHER CONSTANT
ExamplE: When A happens, then B, AND at 
the same time all other factors (C, D, E, …. 
…) are constant (unchanged), then we can 
conclude A causes B
It is easier for nature science to study the 
causality, because control experiment in 
laboratory can be performed
Not that easy or even impossible for social 
science
ECON1220 L1 - Thinking Like an Economist
6
ECON1220_ch2_7:
Potential Problem: Omitted Variable
For example, A and B happens at the same 
time (correlation) and A causes C (causality)
However, you observe only for B and C
If you conclude B causes C, you are wrong
TExTbook ExamplE: p.42
Reported “A strong relationship between: The 
number of cigarette lighters that a household 
owns and the probability that someone in the 
household will develop cancer.”
Policy recommendation: Discourage the 
owning of cigarette lighters by taxes!!!
ECON1220 L1 - Thinking Like an Economist
7
ECON1220_ch2_8:
Potential Problem: Omitted Variable
Think carefully: All other factors are constant?
When you find more lighters, at the same 
time, it means the person smokes or smokes 
more heavily
ECON1220 L1 - Thinking Like an Economist
8
ECON1220_ch2_9:
Potential Problem: Reverse Causality
Which causes which?
Direction as Causality: The variable moves 
first is the cause?  WRONG!
People changes of behavior not based on 
something happened, but expectation on 
something to happen!
ECON1220 L1 - Thinking Like an Economist
9
ECON1220_ch2_10:
Scientific Method
The Scientific Method: Observation, Theory, 
and More Observation (Textbook, p18)
(Most) economists treats Economics a 
natural science, similar to physics
What is Scientific Method?
1) Phenomenon is observed 
2) Explanation is provided by focusing on the 
relevant factors only and ignoring the 
irrelevant ones
Explanation through a “Simplified World” = 
Theory or model
ECON1220 L1 - Thinking Like an Economist
10
ECON1220_ch2_11:
Scientific Method
3) Theory is tested again by real world 
observations / data
Good or bad of a theory or model is judged by its 
explanatory power (how well it explains the 
happened) and predictive power (how well it tells 
the future)
Philosophically, a theory can never be proved to 
be right, but just not yet be rejected
Difficult part: Unlike nature science, “control 
experiments” are uncommon in economics
“Conditions” are impossible to be set exactly 
the same as appear in the model

ECON1220_ch2_15:
Scientific Method
“Assumptions” are used to “keep” our theory 
or model simple
ExamplE: Rationality, PPF (two goods are 
produced in an economy)
Why simple is better?
Assumptions are usually not “real”
The “creative” use of “unreal” assumptions to 
build a good theory should be admired
One more time: Good or bad of a theory or 
model is to be judged by its explanatory and 
predictive power!!!

ECON1220_ch2_16:
Positive VS Normative Analysis
We are human, it is difficult to isolate our own 
value judgement and feeling from analysis
Value judgement and feeling may affect our 
objectiveness in analysis
It does not mean that value and feeling are 
not important, but they should not interfere 
with analysis
It is easier to achieve for nature science, like 
physics and chemistry, but difficult for 
economics
ECON1220 L1 - Thinking Like an Economist
16
ECON1220_ch2_17:
Positive VS Normative Analysis
Normative: “What should be”
Normative statement/analysis involves value-
judgment of the person who make it
Subjective: We should reduce poverty in our 
society
Positive: What it is?
Positive analysis is on “correlation” or “cause-
consequence”
Example: More equal wealth distribution leads to 
higher economic growth
ECON1220 L1 - Thinking Like an Economist
17
ECON1220_ch2_18:
Positive VS Normative analysis
We have to make sure what we are 
discussing
Positive or Normative stuffs?
Positive statement can be tested by 
observations or data (if those are available)

ECON1220_ch2_22:
Why Economists Disagree
Economists have different thoughts!  
Example: The analysis of causes and treatment 
of the 2008 financial crisis
Disagreement could be due to difference in 
scientific judgment
Economists disagree on how much government 
can help
Under some theories, gov’t can have limited help 
or should do nothing (Chicago School )
Problem: Natural experiment is impossible, 
so insufficient data to judge the explanatory 
power of different model/theory precisely
ECON1220 L1 - Thinking Like an Economist
22
ECON1220_ch2_23:
Why Economists Disagree
It can be due to disagreement on value 
judgment:  
Patients should get same quality of treatment, so 
gov’t should spend more money on public 
hospital system
High income group should help the low income 
group.  Our taxation system should be more 
progressive
Values could be subjective (e.g. who is more 
beautiful or handsome), so some economists 
believe Economics should focus only on 
“positive” area (What do you think?)
